entry.title: Goodreads 2018 Reading Challenge
entry.summary:
Books and Books and All Sorts of Books

I like to read. I track my reading habits on Goodreads, and I started using the Goodreads annual Reading Challenge a couple years ago. For 2018 I set out to read 24 books, but I blew past 24 books fairly quickly, so …
entry.content:
Books and Books and All Sorts of Books

I like to read. I track my reading habits on Goodreads, and I started using the Goodreads annual Reading Challenge a couple years ago. For 2018 I set out to read 24 books, but I blew past 24 books fairly quickly, so I changed my goal to 36 books. Down to the wire, I squeezed out the last book on December 31st.

I knocked out an unusually high number of fictional works in 2018. As a huge fan the of the video game The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt, I wanted to read the books upon which the game was based. In a bit of a mad dash to get to 36 books for the year, I started binging the Harry Potter series over the Christmas holiday season.

The Breakdown!

The Good

Books I particularly enjoyed:

  • “Against Democracy” - Jason Brennan

An excellent book for anyone who grew up believing in universal voting rights. You wouldn’t trust your mechanic to perform open heart surgery on you, because they are not qualified to do so. Why do you trust your equally unqualified fellow citizens to exercise voting power over you? Shouldn’t we leave voting to those qualified to vote? You probably find that a somewhat shocking idea. Read the book, and see how you feel about it then!

  • “Suicide of the West” - Jonah Goldberg

Somewhat like “Against Democracy”, Jonah Goldberg’s latest is nother great read for any left-leaning neoliberal shill like myself. And by that I mean any left-leaning person might find themselves a bit challenged by the content. A great book that struck many chords.

  • “The End of Poverty” - Jeffrey D. Sachs

This was an optional but suggested reading for the 2nd year course I took in the Fall of 2018, “ECON 280 Population and Economic Change“. Am I ever glad I read it! While Sachs’ Millenium Villages Project has met some scrutiny, and the results of the MVP project are questionable, the book is still a powerful and positive message about the power of economic development.

A mildly interesting aside: I could not shake the feeling that this book was very familiar. Had I read it before? A few chapters in I realized why it was familiar: Sachs’ work was attacked a number of times in Naomi Klein’s The Shock Doctrine, which I read back in 2006 - 2007. Very interesting to read the “other side” of the story and, on balance, I have to say Klein’s book was built on some serious cherry picking of Sachs’ history. That said, I’m inclined to go back and read The Shock Doctrine again.

  • “Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind” - Yuval Noah Harari

Not much to say … an excellent read. The follow-up book Homo Deus is in my to do pile.

  • “The Charter of Rights and Freedoms” - Ian Greene

I’ve been reading a lot of politics lately. This is a really good read. It summarizes numerous important Charter cases. Critical to understanding the practical implications of the Charter here in Canada.

  • “What Matter Most is How Well You Walk Through the Fire” - Charles Bukowski

I am neither a poet, nor much into poetry. However, on YouTube there is a great mashup of Bukowski’s “Roll the Dice” (read by Tom O’Bedlam) and a weigthlifting insirational video featuring Donny Shankle, former member of the US Weightlifting Team. I bought some of Bukowski’s books as a direct result of that video!

  • “Wherever You Go, There You Are” - Jon Kabat-Zinn

One of the better mindfulness meditation books I have read. Almost completely woo-free, short “chapters” discussing specific points, and includes exercises to assist your practice.

  • “Stubborn Attachments” - Tyler Cowan

I’m a big Tyler Cowan fan and this is a wonderful “positive messages” book about economic growth, morality, rights and obligations.

Mildly interesting aside: This book comes from Stripe Press, and they produce a very small number of books on progressive topics in economics and technology. I did not know this until after I had received my copy, but Stripe Press was created by Stripe, the payments company.

  • “Arguably: Selected Essays” - Christopher Hitchens

I’m an even bigger Hitchens fan than I am a Cowan fan. There is no finer a wordsmith than Christopher Hitchens. His early death is a loss for all of us. Amongst this collection of essays, my favourites are those politically oriented, but his book reviews are also very interesting. I read a ton but I am not really up to speed on “literature”, contemporary or classical. Hitchens’ book reviews provide a lot of fodder for future reading.

  • “The Case Against Education” - Bryan Caplan

Probably my favourite read of the year. With my Daughter in her 3rd year and as a part-time undergrad myself, this was particularly on point.

The Bad

Books that were particularly terrible:

  • “Mindfullness in Plain English” - Henepola Gunaratana

A terrible book on mindfulness, full of woo and little to no practical value.

  • “Bushcraft 101” - Dave Cantebury

I have no idea how this became a NYT best seller. It is not an instructional book. At best it surveys the various bushcraft skills that should prove useful to you should you need them. You will need to look elsewhere if you actually want to learn those skills. My old Scout books were much better.

The Rest

Everything else … all good, all recommended, just not as noteworthy as those mentioned above.

  • “The Dispossessed” Ursula K. Le Guin
  • “A Fire Upon The Deep” - Vernor Vinge
  • “Full Catastrophe Living” - Jon Kabat-Zinn
  • “The Left Hand of Darkness” - Ursula K. Le Guin
  • “The Greatest Story Ever Told - So Far” - Lawrence M. Krauss
  • “The Signal and the Noise” - Nate Silver
  • “The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F*ck” - Mark Manson
  • “Priceless: The Myth of Fair Value” - William Poundstone
  • “Blood of Elves” - Andrzej Sapkowski
  • “Notes From Underground” - Fyodor Dostoyevsky
  • “The Last Wish” - Andrzej Sapkowski
  • “Thoughts Without a Thinker” - Mark Epstein
  • “Sword of Destiny” - Andrzej Sapkowski
  • “The Time of Contempt” - Andrzej Sapkowski
  • “Baptism of Fire” - Andrzej Sapkowski
  • “Us vs. Them: The Failure of Globalism” - Ian Bremmer
  • “Dragonflight” - Anne McCaffrey
  • “Assassin’s Apprentice” - Robin Hobb
  • “Blindsight” - Peter Watts
  • “Democracy and Political Ignorance” - Ilya Somin
  • “Ten Argument for Deleting Your Social Media Accounts Right Now” - Jaron Lanier
  • “Harry Potter and the Philosopher’s Stone” - J.K. Rowling
  • “Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets” - J.K. Rowling
  • “Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban” - J.K. Rowling

Books in Progress

Books I started, and possibly stalled on, in 2018, these will get my 2019 Reading Challenge started:

  • “Advanced Bushcraft” - Dave Cantebury
  • “The American” - Martin Booth
  • “Why” - Samantha Kleinberg
  • “The DevOps Handbook” - Gene Kim
  • “OAuth 2 in Action” - Justin Richer
  • “The Practice of System and Network Administration: Vol. 1” - Thomas A. Limoncelli
  • “Principles: Life and Work” - Ray Dalio
  • “The Neutronium Alchemist” - Peter F. Hamilton
  • “Real Happiness” - Sharon Salzberg
  • “Personal Kanban” - Jim Benson
  • “Capital in the Twenty-First Century” - Thomas Piketty
  • “A Game of Thrones” - George R.R. Martin
  • “The Year’s Best Science Fiction” - Gardner Dozois
  • “The Science of Leadership” - Julian Barling
  • “Site Reliability Engineering” - Betsy Beyer
  • “Google Cloud in Action” - JJ Geewax

entry.title: The Gartner IAM Summit - Day 0
entry.summary: I’m in Las Vegas this week for Gartner’s IAM Summit, being held at Ceasar’s Palace. Vegas isn’t really my cup of tea but one goes where the conferences are. Pro tip: The desert is really freaking cold in December. I mean, not Canada cold, but way …
entry.content:
I’m in Las Vegas this week for Gartner’s IAM Summit, being held at Ceasar’s Palace. Vegas isn’t really my cup of tea but one goes where the conferences are. Pro tip: The desert is really freaking cold in December. I mean, not Canada cold, but way colder than a desert should be.

I flew in yesterday, which gave me a leasurely day today for registration, and allowed me to attend a couple “tutorials” as well as the welcome reception. The tutorials were just talks, not apparentyl different from any other conference session I have attended. There was certainly nothing hands-on about them. I peeled out of the welcome reception pretty quick, welcome receptions also not really being my thing. And I do have an exam to study for.

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 13
entry.summary:
Salaberry-de-Valleyfield

Distance traveled: 240kms

Home Sweet Home
entry.content:
Salaberry-de-Valleyfield

Distance traveled: 240kms

Home Sweet Home

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 12
entry.summary:
Edmundston to Salaberry-de-Valleyfield

Distance traveled (approximate): 611kms

Almost homeward bound. More of those gorgeous inter-New Brunswick - Quebec views.

On our way to our last motel, we stopped at the massive Carrier Harley / Can-Am / BMW Motorrad shop in Drummondville. These guys had everything, even the BMW Motorrad bikini, flip-flops, and board …
entry.content:
Edmundston to Salaberry-de-Valleyfield

Distance traveled (approximate): 611kms

Almost homeward bound. More of those gorgeous inter-New Brunswick - Quebec views.

On our way to our last motel, we stopped at the massive Carrier Harley / Can-Am / BMW Motorrad shop in Drummondville. These guys had everything, even the BMW Motorrad bikini, flip-flops, and board shorts.

From there it was off to Salaberry-de-Valleyfield. The motel we were supposed to book into turned out to be garbage. The first and only crap motel on this entire trip. Well, the Brockville motel my travelling companions stayed in back on day 1 was pretty gross too. Anyway, this place was pretty terrible. No ground floor access, a room decidedly on the lower end of the spectrum, and it absolutely reeked of cigarette smoke

After a very painful experience dealing with Hotels.com we were able to cancel the booking and we moved to the Hôtel Plaza Valleyfield. Much better! Clean and comfortable room with a very nice Italian restaurant downstairs. It was hotel, so we had to truck our stuff upstairs, but it was worth it. Our last night on the road … clean sheets, hot showers, and some excellent dining.

We tried walking off the pasta a bit by the waterfront. Here’s me in my sparkly “Pink Lady Slipper Tour” t-shirt from our 2014 trip to P.E.I.

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 11
entry.summary:
Antigonish to Moncton to Edmundston.

Distacnce traveled (approximate): 718kms

Today was a very long haul, with a somewhat unplanned detour. The Harley was very clearly spitting oil out the right side so on our way to Edmunstun, we headed into Moncton to visit Toys for Big Boys Harley-Davidson. Apparently the …
entry.content:
Antigonish to Moncton to Edmundston.

Distacnce traveled (approximate): 718kms

Today was a very long haul, with a somewhat unplanned detour. The Harley was very clearly spitting oil out the right side so on our way to Edmunstun, we headed into Moncton to visit Toys for Big Boys Harley-Davidson. Apparently the bike had too much oil in it and the motor slowly spits the excesss out the air filter. Also apparently if the air filter becomes sufficiently saturated with oil, it creates a vacuum condition that just keeps sucking more oil out of the motor. All of this sounds completely bonkers.

Anyway, the service tech at Toys for Big Boys was great, and got the Harley squared away in short order. The name “Toys for Big Boys” is pretty lame though.

An extra long ride always calls for beer and sunshine. Days Inn Edmundston.

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 10
entry.summary: Channel-Port aux Basques to Antigonish. We altered our return ferry to cross in the daytime. We also got lucky and we able to upgrade to a berth so we could at least try to nap. Still not the best way to do the crossing on motorcycles since you are still …
entry.content: Channel-Port aux Basques to Antigonish. We altered our return ferry to cross in the daytime. We also got lucky and we able to upgrade to a berth so we could at least try to nap. Still not the best way to do the crossing on motorcycles since you are still tired and it is late in the day when you land so we were racing to our next motel, Homeward Inns of Canada, another great motorcycle friendly location.

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 9
entry.summary:
From the Lodge to Port aux Basques.

Distance traveled (approximate): 528kms

And we’re off! We’ve made some adjustments to our return plans. We will spend the night in Channel-Port aux Basques and then take a day crossing back to North Sydney. Ideally we would then spend a night …
entry.content:
From the Lodge to Port aux Basques.

Distance traveled (approximate): 528kms

And we’re off! We’ve made some adjustments to our return plans. We will spend the night in Channel-Port aux Basques and then take a day crossing back to North Sydney. Ideally we would then spend a night in North Syndey, but we’ve decided to press on to Antigonish.

I just love a packed and trip ready Beemer.

A little scenery.

We’re spending the night at St. Christopher’s. A decent enough hotel, with priority motorcycle parking, but don’t bother with the fish n’ chips. It’s not good!

The dying light in Port aux Basques.

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 8
entry.summary: An honest-to-goodness day off. Well, sort of. We start our trip back home tomorrow so there was all the packing and pre-ride checks to do. Still, mostly a day of patio sitting!
entry.content: An honest-to-goodness day off. Well, sort of. We start our trip back home tomorrow so there was all the packing and pre-ride checks to do. Still, mostly a day of patio sitting!

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 7
entry.summary:
From the Lodge to Mount Pearl and back!

Distance traveled (approximate): 734kms

It’s Pick Up the Harley Day! My buddy DreadPirateRoberts and his Father-in-Law took the four-wheeled cage and I followed along on my Beemer. It’s a long haul to St. John’s / Mount Pearl and back but …
entry.content:
From the Lodge to Mount Pearl and back!

Distance traveled (approximate): 734kms

It’s Pick Up the Harley Day! My buddy DreadPirateRoberts and his Father-in-Law took the four-wheeled cage and I followed along on my Beemer. It’s a long haul to St. John’s / Mount Pearl and back but I was itching to ride and I sure as heck was going to drive back in a car while DreadPirateRoberts ride his Harley back.

While they were at Rugged Rock Harley I headed over to The Toy Box / Avalon Motorrad and picked up an Avalon Motorrad t-shirt.

I heade back to Rugged Rock. DreadPirateRoberts bought me a Harley tank top. I guess we have a new rule … if a bike breaks down causing a disruption in out travels plans, the owner of said bike must by everyone else some swag. Which is fine by me. Being on a BMW I’ll never need to buy anyone else swag.

On the way back to the Lodge I got a pic by the Gander sign. Earlier in the year my Mother-in-Law had taken my Wife and I to see “Come From Away” so it was fun to get a picture.

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 6
entry.summary: With the Harley in the shop getting fixed up we took one of those four wheeled vehicular contraptions to do some touring. We took the 340 North to the Beothuk Interpretation Centre (the Beothuk were the indigenous population in Newfoundland). The picture below is the waterfront at the Interpretaion Centre …
entry.content:
With the Harley in the shop getting fixed up we took one of those four wheeled vehicular contraptions to do some touring. We took the 340 North to the Beothuk Interpretation Centre (the Beothuk were the indigenous population in Newfoundland). The picture below is the waterfront at the Interpretaion Centre.

From there we went to the Long Point Lighthouse. The Lighthouse was closed but the scenery is pretty amazing.

Repping Auth0 on The Rock.

On our way back we stopped for some lobster. Lobster season was over but we were able to find som decent sized critters for dinner.

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 5
entry.summary: With my buddy’s bike broken down, and us waiting on Rugged Rock Harley-Davidson to come pick it up, I headed out for a little ride. I went to Gander, and then took the 330 North to Musgrave Harbour. I had originally planned to take the 330 all the way …
entry.content:
With my buddy’s bike broken down, and us waiting on Rugged Rock Harley-Davidson to come pick it up, I headed out for a little ride. I went to Gander, and then took the 330 North to Musgrave Harbour. I had originally planned to take the 330 all the way around to Gambo and then back to the Lodge, but by the time I got to Musgrave Harbour I wasn’t feeling it. Always a good sign that you should turn around, go home, and put your feet up.

The shop foreman from Rugged Rock showed up later that afternoon, had a look at the bike, loaded it in his trailor, and hung out at the Lodge with us for a short bit before heading back to Mount Pearl. I should point out it is a full 8 hour day to drive from Mount Pearl to the Lodge and back!

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 4
entry.summary:
Channel-Port aux Basques to the Lodge

Distance traveled (approximate): 529kms

Pulling into Channel-Port aux Basques.

It turned out to be a pretty eventful day. The weather when we got off the ferry was terrible. Heavy rain and transport trucks on the Trans-Canada Highway lead to lots of spray and dicey …
entry.content:
Channel-Port aux Basques to the Lodge

Distance traveled (approximate): 529kms

Pulling into Channel-Port aux Basques.

It turned out to be a pretty eventful day. The weather when we got off the ferry was terrible. Heavy rain and transport trucks on the Trans-Canada Highway lead to lots of spray and dicey riding conditions.

The weather slowly let up and we started to get some sunshine, but then my travelling companion’s motorcycle started having serious difficulties. Significant clouds of white smoke at slow speeds, alarmingly high fuel consumption, and lack of motor power on the highway.

Exhausted from our poorly planned ferry ride and despite a very sick Harley, we made it to the Lodge. A much looked foward to meal!

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 3
entry.summary:
Moncton to the North Sydney Ferry Terminal.

Distance traveled (approximate): 481kms

After getting to Cape Breton, about half way to North Sydney is Big Spruce Brewing, a small local brewery high on a hill overlooking the water. Well worth the visit. I don’t really have a decent picture that …
entry.content:
Moncton to the North Sydney Ferry Terminal.

Distance traveled (approximate): 481kms

After getting to Cape Breton, about half way to North Sydney is Big Spruce Brewing, a small local brewery high on a hill overlooking the water. Well worth the visit. I don’t really have a decent picture that doesn’t include my travelling companions, and I don’t post pictures of them! The next photo is on our way “down” into North Sydney.

In line for the ferry to Channel-Port aux Basques. We took an overnight ferry, which turned out to be a huge mistake!

It me! We were up in the premium seating, but in seats nonetheless (there were no berths available). Needless to say, sleep was fitful at best, part of the reason an overnight ferry turned out to be a terrible idea. A long day on a motorcycle after a crap sleep is not recommended.

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 2
entry.summary:
Saint-Jean-Port-Joli to Moncton

Distance traveled (approximate): 640kms

From Saint-Jean-Port-Joli we made our way to Moncton. The ride between Quebec City area all the way into New Brunswick is gorgeous. I kinda regret not taking my GoPro, but video logging a motorcycle trip is a pile of work. This first picture …
entry.content:
Saint-Jean-Port-Joli to Moncton

Distance traveled (approximate): 640kms

From Saint-Jean-Port-Joli we made our way to Moncton. The ride between Quebec City area all the way into New Brunswick is gorgeous. I kinda regret not taking my GoPro, but video logging a motorcycle trip is a pile of work. This first picture is from shortly after we rode into New Brunswick. It was bit damp, but that’s what the Gore-Tex is for. That’s me up front.

The Amsterdam Inn & Suites was another winner. After 600+ kilometers of riding, it’s pretty satisfying to pull up right in front of the door to a clean and comfortable room for the night.

entry.title: The Viking Tour - Day 1
entry.summary:
Home to Saint-Jean-Port-Joli

Distance traveled (approximate): 635kms

So once a year a buddy and I, along with his wife, head off on one long motorcycle trip. Last year was “The Moose Tour” to Bar Harbour, Maine, with another riding buddy in tow. This year it’s to be The Viking …
entry.content:
Home to Saint-Jean-Port-Joli

Distance traveled (approximate): 635kms

So once a year a buddy and I, along with his wife, head off on one long motorcycle trip. Last year was “The Moose Tour” to Bar Harbour, Maine, with another riding buddy in tow. This year it’s to be The Viking Tour to The Rock. My buddy’s wife is from Newfoundland, and her parents run a vacation lodge up around Lewisporte. Yes, a trip to Newfoundland should have been called “The Moose Tour” and Bar Harbour might better have been “The Lobster Tour”. This will be, by a pretty decent margin, our longest trip yet, running about 5,400kms for me.

Today I headed to Brockville to meet up with the team. Then we were off to Saint-Jean-Port-Joli for our first night. Our first motel was a winner. The Motel Blanche d’Haberville is a motorcyclist friendly location.

Just around the corner from the Blache d’Haberville is the Pub Ras L’Bock. Patio dining, quality local beer, great food, waterfront views, all within walking distance of the motel.

entry.title: I Love New Tires (and Routine Maintenance)
entry.summary: Closing in on 20,000kms on my 2016 R1200GS, and with my moto trip to Newfoundland looming, I booked the bike in for its 20,000km service. I really enjoy taking my vehicles in for routine maintenance. It’s surprisingly satisfying. Even more satisfying is new tires. I just loving …
entry.content:
Closing in on 20,000kms on my 2016 R1200GS, and with my moto trip to Newfoundland looming, I booked the bike in for its 20,000km service. I really enjoy taking my vehicles in for routine maintenance. It’s surprisingly satisfying. Even more satisfying is new tires. I just loving putting new tires on my motorbike and my car.

On my old FZ6 I replaced the stock tires with Metzeler Roadtec Z8 Interacts, which I was quite happy with. So I decided to replace my GS’s stock Anakee III tires with the new Metzeler Roadtec 01s. The Roadtecs are so much smoother than the Anakees, especially at freeway speeds, that is it a little bit hard to believe. Granted the Anakees are a 90/10 tire and the Roadtecs are pure road, but what a difference.

I had the work done at Endras BMW Motorrad in Ajax, Ontario. The service tech there was great. I mentioned the small amount of oil on my left fork. The clip that holds the seal in place (I really have no idea what I am talking about here) was not installed correctly, so the service tech fixed it up and told me to keep an eye out for any further leakage.

entry.title: Forking Seals
entry.summary: The quick fix on the left fork seal did not work. Not long after leaving the shop you could see fork oil slowly accumulating on top of the seal and on the fork itself. I called Endras back and they booked my in for Friday the 28th. Hopefully last minute …
entry.content:
The quick fix on the left fork seal did not work. Not long after leaving the shop you could see fork oil slowly accumulating on top of the seal and on the fork itself. I called Endras back and they booked my in for Friday the 28th. Hopefully last minute maintenance before a big Summer motorcycle trip doesn’t become a habit.

Minor panic when I got the bike in. The tech who was scheduled to do the work was off sick. Now, I must say Endras got me fixed up. They understood the urgency and got one of their other service techs to squeeze it into their schedule. On the other hand, a call to let me know my tech was off sick would have been appreciated!

Still, all’s well that ends well. The work was covered under warranty and I was all set for my Newfoundland trip. I also picked up the BMW Small Tankrucksack while I was there.

entry.title: Cloud and Identity Systems Lead
entry.summary: A new shift in my responsibilities at work, with more focus on two key aspects of our environment that I have been leading on the operations side of things: Consolidating our cloud computing resources on Google Cloud Platform and our migration to our new Identity as a Service partner, Auth0 …
entry.content:
A new shift in my responsibilities at work, with more focus on two key aspects of our environment that I have been leading on the operations side of things: Consolidating our cloud computing resources on Google Cloud Platform and our migration to our new Identity as a Service partner, Auth0.

This is a welcome change. The nature of my work is such that I tend to be pulled in many different directions at once (this is what happens when you are interested in and develop expertise in everything under the Sun). That is still the case now, but at least recognizing these two keys areas does help to stay on target.

entry.title: Linux Foundation Certified Engineer
entry.summary: Renewal time for my LFCS certification. The Linux Foundation will renew your LFCS certification for 2 years if you upgrade to the LFCE cert, so that’s what I did!
entry.content: Renewal time for my LFCS certification. The Linux Foundation will renew your LFCS certification for 2 years if you upgrade to the LFCE cert, so that’s what I did!

entry.title: I Broke My GS - Update
entry.summary: One of the service reps at my dealership went to bat for me and got me squared away. My GS will be all fixed up in plenty of time for my trip to Maine.
entry.content: One of the service reps at my dealership went to bat for me and got me squared away. My GS will be all fixed up in plenty of time for my trip to Maine.

entry.title: I Broke My GS
entry.summary:
I broke my GS.

No injuries other than my pride. Less than a second’s worth of inattention and I dropped my bike at a gas station while I was out joy riding this past Sunday (July 16th). The resulting chaos was impressive for such a silly little tip-over.

I …
entry.content:
I broke my GS.

No injuries other than my pride. Less than a second’s worth of inattention and I dropped my bike at a gas station while I was out joy riding this past Sunday (July 16th). The resulting chaos was impressive for such a silly little tip-over.

I had taken back roads from Kingston all the way out to Hawkesbury and was returning via highway 17. There was a convenient gas station and Tim’s in Alfred. A top up would get me home without stopping and it was time for some water and a snack. Gas station was first so I pulled up to a pump.

This is where the less than a second of inattention comes in. I didn’t ensure my kick stand was fully engaged before I started to lean the bike over. And down it went. One moment I’m looking at the price of Premium and the next moment I’m staring at my toppled over moto underneath me. I admit I was confused for a bit.

Well, an opportunity to work on my deadlift. Initially it seemed like a total non-event. Some minor scuffs on the plastic. Sad, but it is a GS after all. Everything looked to be in order so I proceeded to fill up. Yes, I should have confirmed it was operable first! Paid for my gas (it was one of those gas stations), got geared back up, jumped in the saddle and turned on the power. Motion by my left foot caught my eye. I looked down to see gasoline gushing out of my motorcycle.

I was a little stunned. In some sort of state of shock. With my wits mostly going off in various directions I turned off the power and wheeled the bike out of the way. Had a closer look, removed a piece of ‘protective’ bodywork and saw the damage. The exterior portion of my fuel injector had been sheered off!

As it turned out, when the bike tipped over, it was positioned so that the cylinder head fell just beside the pump’s concrete island, which allowed the lip of the concrete island to impact the fuel injector assembly, which is protected only by a heavy plastic cover. Not much protection when the weight of the bike is laying on it. Had the bike been an inch or so closer or farther from the pump, I’d have escaped with just the scratched plastics.

I was 250kms from home.

CAA to the Rescue? More or Less…

I called CAA at 6:16PM. They were busy so it was a bit of a wait before someone could take my call. After initially logging the call they had to transfer me to the “Ontario East” region so I was on hold again. Finally by 7:27PM a truck was on its way to pick me up!

Just kidding. That’s just what the text message from CAA said. At around 7:53PM the dispatcher called to see if the truck had arrived. No. Ok, so they’ll try calling the driver and get back to me. Text message with an ETA update.

8:12PM - Dispatch calls to see if the truck has arrived. Nope. Oh… we’re having trouble getting in touch with the driver. We’ll get back to you. Text message with an ETA update.

8:34PM - Dispatch calls to see if the truck has arrived. Nien. The driver is not answering his phone. So send another bloody truck! But dispatch insists on trying once more. It’s getting dark and starting to rain.

45 minutes goes by with not a word from dispatch. So I call CAA again. On hold again. Transferred to East again. On hold again. Finally speaking to another dispatcher and they too try calling the original driver but aren’t having any luck.

9:38PM - Finally dispatch sends out a new driver with a 40 minute ETA. But Lady Luck was smiling upon me that day! The new driver is there in about 20 minutes. This kid was great. He got me squared away real quick. We stopped at that Tim’s I was going to stop at 3+ hours ago for some coffee, and had a nice chat with some random moto guy whose daughter actually works at my dealership.

An hour later we dropped the bike off at my dealership in Nepean. The driver was kind enough to drop me off at my hotel. I suspect the dispatcher told him to go the extra mile on this call. Still, kudos to that young lad from Leduc towing, and a pox on CAA and the guy from Garage Lamarche who was supposed to pick me up. Whose shop was, by the by, 1 minute down the road from the gas station where I was stuck!

So, first call at 6:19PM. Dropped the bike off at around Midnight. Around 6 hours to move a motorcycle 83kms. CAA dispatch should not have waited anywhere near so long before sending another driver. And I shouldn’t have waited so long to start telling them to send another driver.

At the Dealership

So the next day (Monday, July 17th), I was at the dealership well before opening. Once the shop opened I got my bike logged in and was immediately told it would be a day or two before it even gets looked at it. I was a little dismayed. I asked the guy at the desk if someone could at just take a quick look. Which he did. Which wasn’t very helpful. Their BMW mechanic could have taken a 30 second glance and told me what he though.

For the record, if you’re a Harley-Davidson owner and you break down while traveling the nearest H-D dealership will put your bike at the front of the line for assessment and, if possible, repair. I really truly completely love my GS, but BMW could learn something here.

So I caught a cab to the train station and booked a lift home. While waiting I called BMW to ask if they offered anything like priority service for travelers. Nien. That’s up to each dealership. I called the dealership to ask the same. I was assured the bike would be looked at and I’d have an estimate before the end of day but it was definitely not going to be fixed that day. I never did get that call.

I did get a call this morning (Tuesday) with an estimate. The estimate was less than I feared, but some of the parts would need to be ordered in from Germany, with a delivery time of 7 - 10 days. I forgot to ask if those were weekdays or business days. Also, I am supposed to be leaving on a trip to Maine, USA, in ten days. Oh dear… I called back. One of the service people offered to try getting the parts from BMW in the US, but it didn’t sound promising. Again, promised a call before the end of the day, but no such call was to be had.

entry.title: And My Axe!
entry.summary:
I bought a guitar: An Epiphone Les Paul Standard PlusTop Pro in “Bourbon Burst” colour scheme. And it is freakin’ gorgeous.

I’ve started hacking on some basics, chords and chord transitions, various forms of scales, and lots of YouTube lessons (Lessons with Cameron, Andy Guitar, and others). Nothing formal …
entry.content:
I bought a guitar: An Epiphone Les Paul Standard PlusTop Pro in “Bourbon Burst” colour scheme. And it is freakin’ gorgeous.

I’ve started hacking on some basics, chords and chord transitions, various forms of scales, and lots of YouTube lessons (Lessons with Cameron, Andy Guitar, and others). Nothing formal for now. If I stick with it for a few weeks then maybe I’ll consider formal lessons. Life goals: to be able to shred along to my favourite metal and punk riffs.

entry.title: My 2016 BMW R1200GS
entry.summary:
Like all right thinking peoples, I like to ride the train. And I particularly like riding the train when there’s a brand new motorcycle waiting for me at the other end of the line.

Of course, a good book is a great way to pass the time if the …
entry.content:
Like all right thinking peoples, I like to ride the train. And I particularly like riding the train when there’s a brand new motorcycle waiting for me at the other end of the line.

Of course, a good book is a great way to pass the time if the scenery isn’t grabbing you.

It makes me very, very happy to have this in my driveway:

Niggles

As I have mentioned elsewhere, the turn signal switch has very little feel to it.

Also, the fuel neck is quite long, making it very difficult to tell when the tank is full.

Really hard to come up with negatives with this bike!

entry.title: AWS Certified Solutions Architect - Associate
entry.summary: Another certification in the bag. I wrote the AWS Associate level Solutions Architect exam today. I didn’t realize AWS certs are only good for 2 years. Sets a bit of a roadmap for my AWS certifications going forward. I got my Associate level SysOps cert in earl 2015, so …
entry.content: Another certification in the bag. I wrote the AWS Associate level Solutions Architect exam today. I didn’t realize AWS certs are only good for 2 years. Sets a bit of a roadmap for my AWS certifications going forward. I got my Associate level SysOps cert in earl 2015, so I’ll want to replace it with the Professional level DevOps cert next year, and I’ll want to replace my Associate level Architect cert with the Professional level cert in 2018.

entry.title: A New Ride - the BMW R1200GS
entry.summary:
A Winner [Updated]

I had a 2017 BMW R1200GS in “Frozen Dark Blue Metallic” on order with a variety of gizmos including BMW’s Navigator V GPS system, keyless ride, and the Akrapovic exhaust. Being only 172cm tall, I was getting the bike lowered to better fit my height.

My …
entry.content:
A Winner [Updated]

I had a 2017 BMW R1200GS in “Frozen Dark Blue Metallic” on order with a variety of gizmos including BMW’s Navigator V GPS system, keyless ride, and the Akrapovic exhaust. Being only 172cm tall, I was getting the bike lowered to better fit my height.

My dealer recently contacted me to say they had a 2016 model available thanks to another buyer who backed out of the deal. Still in its crate, same colour, all the factory options and the lowering kit installed, and it can be ready for me in a week or two.

I Ride

I received my motorcycle license in the Fall of 2011. I also bought my first and, until now, only motorcycle at the same time: a previously enjoyed 2006 Yamaha FZ6 sport touring bike. The previous owners didn’t do much with it, there being only 9,000kms on it at the time of purchase. Since then I’ve added about 40,000kms to the odo. I commute to work by motorcycle most days from March until well into December, do occasional joy rides on the weekends, and I love long distance motorcycle touring.

The FZ6 is a blast to ride and I love it now as much, and possibly even more, than ever. However, while it is a sport touring rig, it lacks certain creature comforts for the long road, like cruise control and power outlets. The after-market saddle I bought is good for 1.5 to 2 hours at a time. Not bad, but 2+ hours would be better. Some of the missing features on the FZ6 can be added (power, heated grips) but I want to upgrade to something with modern technology and touring features as stock.

I’ve been shopping new bikes for a year or more now and recently narrowed the field of options to a single mid-size bike, the Triumph Tiger 800 XRT, and four heavyweights:

  • Yamaha FJR1300
  • Triumph Tiger Explorer 1200
  • BMW R1200RT
  • BMW R1200GS

Triumph Tiger 800

I was able to ride a Tiger 800 demo bike for a good 5 hours and it is just fantastic. If you like that style of bike, you’d be hard pressed to find something else that offers everything the 800 XRT offers. It is a blast to ride and has both cruise control and heated seats, the later being a first in mid-sized bikes as far as I know.

I had looked at Yamaha’s FJ-09 as well, but it lacks cruise control, which is a deal breaker for me. I suspect Yamaha will add cruise for 2017 (it is on the new for 2017 FZ-10) but no matter, I have already decided to go with a heavyweight bike. One nice feature of the FJ-09 over the Tiger: adjustable suspension. Why Triumph did not include the adjustable WP suspension on the XR models of the 800, when it is included in the XC models, is beyond me.

I had actually put a deposit down on a 2017 Tiger 800 XRT, but I knew the mid-size bike was going to be a temporary measure on my way to an eventual heavyweight. I decided to skip the half-step and go straight to the heavy, in part because, why finance the 800 now, only to finance a heavyweight later? I had, and maybe still have, some concerns about using the larger bike for daily commuting. Nonetheless, I’ve decided to go with the heavy.

Yamaha FJR1300

My experiences with the FZ6 have made me somewhat of a “Yamaha guy”. I really want to love the FJR. It is drop-dead gorgeous, and a fantastic ride by all accounts (I haven’t ridden it myself). But it is old. The basic platform was introduced in 2001. Yamaha has made incremental changes to the bike over the years, including some very cool changes to the gearbox for 2016, but it still shows its age in features like the very basic ride mode technology. If Yamaha gave the FJR a full reboot, I would be giving it a good deal more attention.

The Beemers

I took advantage of BMW’s demo roadshow on multiple occasions this year to ride the K1600GT, the R1200RT (twice), and the R1200GS (twice). The K was great, but just too much power for my liking. It’s a race bike in full touring rig. Most recently I rode the RT and the GS back to back, and without any doubt I found the GS to be both more comfortable and more rideable for me.

I had some specific issues with the RT. The front end seemed to waver at low speeds (30 - 50kph). I don’t remember that happening the first time I rode the RT, but it was a consistent issue the second time around. And there is a ton of vibration in handlebars at 4500 - 5000RPM which is a rev range you frequently find yourself in. The GS had neither of these problems. It’s only ‘issue’ is its height. It is super-tall in its stock setup, and I am not! I did the demo rides with the low seat and that was manageable, but I am going with the lowering kit option which will allow me to use the low or regular seat. The low seat will be good for stop-and-go commuter traffic, while the regular height seat will provide extra comfort on long trips. Mind you, I found the low seat to be very comfortable in the first place.

There is one very annoying issue with all the BMWs: The switch gear on the handlebars has little to no tactile feedback, in particular the turn signal switch. With gloves on you cannot feel the switch moving and there is no affirative ‘click’ when the signal is engaged. You actually have to look at your hand to see if you are pushing the switch properly, or actively check the display to confirm the signal is on. I’ve played with the switch gear on almost every BMW bike model and they are all the same. It is more than a little ridiculous that BMW would mess something like that up.

Triumph Tiger Explorer 1200

Unfortunately Triumph does not offer any sort of demo program for the Explorer in Canada. While I am sure the Explorer is a very good bike (generally very positive reviews all around) and despite a strong personal affinity for the Triumph brand, the inability to ride a demo bike was a major factor in my decision to go with the BMW. Maybe I’ll take a trip to the UK at some point and take an Explorer out for a spin, but for now my decision is made, and I am bloody excited!

As an aside: I actually emailed Triumph “Canada” about the lack of demo programs and the role that played in my decision. It turns out:

  1. The contact email address on the Triump Canada web site actually goes to Triumph USA, and

  2. Triumph USA didn’t much care about my story, telling me they “gave up the demo trucks in 2006”, downloading responsibility for demos to the dealers and that I should “contact [my] local Triumph dealer”.

I’m guessing sales isn’t particularly high on Triumph’s agenda on this side of the pond? Would you be inclined to buy a ~$25,000 motorcycle without riding it first?

entry.title: Platform and Data Operations Analyst
entry.summary: In what was more than a bit of a surprise, I was “laterally promoted” to a new role with my employer, something I only found out less than 24 hours before it was announced to the department. I shall not complain. A bit of an odd way to go about …
entry.content:
In what was more than a bit of a surprise, I was “laterally promoted” to a new role with my employer, something I only found out less than 24 hours before it was announced to the department. I shall not complain. A bit of an odd way to go about things from a human resources perspective, but I’m very happy about my new position, and very excited about what lies ahead.

My somewhat nebulous new title is “Platform and Data Operations Analyst”. Good descriptors for what I will actually be doing include “cloud guy” and “putting the Ops in DevOps”. Having previously taken lead on some of our department’s cloud initiatives, I’m being given the chance to do cloud operations full-time. Which is bloody fantastic. But I think they should have put “Cloud” in front of “Platform”.

As the title suggests, there is also a data portion to this. I have been doing some very junior level work with our Data Services team for the past year or so, and I hope to continue doing more, though the details still need to be hammered out. It is possible my new position will lead me more toward ‘data engineering’, helping our data team leverage cloud-based data services. The door is certainly open for me to spend more of my time on data / analytics.

entry.title: Linux Foundation Certified System Administrator
entry.summary: I’ve been at this Unix and Linux systems management stuff for over 20 years now. As with my undergraduate degree1, I’ve long wanted to knock out a few relevant technology certifications. Following on from my AWS SysOps certification, I took and passed the Linux Foundation’s LFCS …
entry.content:
I’ve been at this Unix and Linux systems management stuff for over 20 years now. As with my undergraduate degree1, I’ve long wanted to knock out a few relevant technology certifications. Following on from my AWS SysOps certification, I took and passed the Linux Foundation’s LFCS certification exam this week.

  1. I am a part-time undergrad at my local University, chipping away at a B.A. in Economics. ↩

entry.title: My First Pull Request
entry.summary:
I use Pelican and the pelican-bootstrap3 theme to built this site.

I made my first real pull request today in the form of a minor contribution to pelican-bootstrap3. I added a feature that allows you to add a series of images to the sidebar. The original intent was to allow …
entry.content:
I use Pelican and the pelican-bootstrap3 theme to built this site.

I made my first real pull request today in the form of a minor contribution to pelican-bootstrap3. I added a feature that allows you to add a series of images to the sidebar. The original intent was to allow me to add certification marks to my sidebar, which you can now see in action right here!

Props to @DandyDev for the pro tips.

entry.title: AWS SysOps Administration - Associate
entry.summary: Last week I took the AWS SysOps Administrator - Associate test and passed with flying colours. Next step will be the Developer and then the DevOps certifications, both of which I hope to complete this year. I look forward to doing a lot more work in AWS in the future.
entry.content: Last week I took the AWS SysOps Administrator - Associate test and passed with flying colours. Next step will be the Developer and then the DevOps certifications, both of which I hope to complete this year. I look forward to doing a lot more work in AWS in the future.

entry.title: My First Kaggle Competition
entry.summary:
We are wrapping up the final week of MIT’s 15.071x “The Analytics Edge” course, delivered online via edX.org.

As part of the course I participated in my first Kaggle competition. I finished in 1406th place out of 2923 people with a Private leaderboard score of 0.88319 …
entry.content:
We are wrapping up the final week of MIT’s 15.071x “The Analytics Edge” course, delivered online via edX.org.

As part of the course I participated in my first Kaggle competition. I finished in 1406th place out of 2923 people with a Private leaderboard score of 0.88319, down from a public leaderboard score of 0.91792.

The mean score for all submissions was 0.8551523, median 0.90237, with a standard deviation of 0.09301734.

Min.    1st Qu. Median  Mean    3rd Qu. Max.
0.2500  0.7945  0.9024  0.8552  0.9204  0.9455

Not a great result, but a great learning experience. Many of the top finishers are sharing their code so I look forward to reviewing what they did. It is interesting to see some of the dramatic shifts in the top tier finishers when going from the publicly available test data to the private test data set.

My code is here.

The red and blue lines below represent my final and public leaderboard scores respectively.
